original_title: Expressing opposition to Central Business District Tolling Program of New York City.
plain_title: Congress Opposes New York City's Toll Fee for Downtown Manhattan
plain_summary: This resolution is Congress officially saying it disagrees with New York City's plan to charge drivers a fee to enter the busiest part of Manhattan. The program, called congestion pricing, would make drivers pay a toll to reduce traffic in that area. Congress is asking New York City and federal agencies to stop the program and study how it would hurt the economy first.
key_provisions: Congress officially opposes New York City's congestion pricing toll program
Calls for an economic impact study before the program moves forward
Recommends that federal agencies and New York City pause the program
who_it_affects: This bill would affect everyday drivers, commuters, and businesses in and around Manhattan who would otherwise have to pay a new toll fee to drive into the busiest part of New York City.
status_plain_english: This resolution was recently introduced and has been sent to a House committee for review, but has not been voted on yet.

original_title: Reserved for the Speaker.
plain_title: A Placeholder Bill Reserved for the Speaker of the House
plain_summary: This is a placeholder slot in Congress that the Speaker of the House has set aside, likely to introduce an important bill soon. No actual bill text or details have been filled in yet. Think of it like a saved seat at a table — something is coming, but we don't know what it is yet.
key_provisions: No provisions available yet
Bill content has not been released
Speaker has reserved this slot for future legislation
who_it_affects: No one can be identified as affected yet because the bill has no content — once the Speaker fills in the details, we will know who it impacts.
status_plain_english: This bill is just a placeholder and has no content or actions yet — the Speaker of the House is saving this spot to introduce a bill later.

original_title: Reserved for the Speaker.
plain_title: A Placeholder Bill Reserved for the House Speaker
plain_summary: This is a placeholder slot in Congress that the Speaker of the House has set aside for future use. It does not yet contain any actual legislation or policy proposals. Think of it like a saved seat — the Speaker is holding this spot to introduce a bill later.
key_provisions: No provisions exist yet
Content to be determined by the Speaker
Placeholder for future legislation
who_it_affects: No one is affected yet, as this bill has no content — it is simply a reserved slot for the Speaker to fill with a real proposal later.
status_plain_english: This bill has no content or actions yet — it is just a placeholder being held by the Speaker of the House for future use.

original_title: Reserved for the Minority Leader.
plain_title: A Bill Held for the Minority Party Leader to Name
plain_summary: This is a placeholder bill that has been reserved for the Minority Leader of Congress to use when they are ready to introduce legislation. It does not yet have any specific content or purpose. Think of it like a saved spot in line — the details will be filled in later.
key_provisions: No provisions defined yet
Content to be determined by Minority Leader
Placeholder status only
who_it_affects: No one is affected yet, as this bill has no content — it is simply a reserved slot for future legislation.
status_plain_english: This bill is just a placeholder and has not been introduced, debated, or voted on yet.

original_title: Reserved for the Minority Leader.
plain_title: A Placeholder Bill Reserved for the Minority Leader
plain_summary: This is a placeholder slot in Congress reserved for the minority leader, which is the top member of the political party that has fewer seats in that chamber. No actual bill has been written or introduced here yet. Think of it like a saved seat at a lunch table — the spot is held, but no one is sitting there yet.
key_provisions: No provisions written yet
Bill content has not been introduced
Reserved as a placeholder for future legislation
who_it_affects: No one is affected yet because this is just a reserved slot with no actual bill or policy attached to it.
status_plain_english: This is not a real bill yet — it is simply a placeholder reserved for the minority leader to use when they are ready to introduce legislation.

original_title: Reserved for the Minority Leader.
plain_title: A Placeholder Bill Saved for the Minority Leader
plain_summary: This is a reserved bill slot that has been set aside for the Minority Leader of Congress to use when they are ready to introduce a bill. Right now, there is no actual law or policy proposal attached to it. Think of it like a saved seat — the spot exists, but no one is sitting in it yet.
key_provisions: No specific provisions have been written yet
Bill content is pending the Minority Leader's proposal
Slot is reserved as a procedural placeholder
who_it_affects: No one is affected yet because this bill has no content — it is simply a reserved space in the legislative process.
status_plain_english: This bill is just a placeholder and has not been introduced, written, or voted on yet.

original_title: Reserved for the Minority Leader.
plain_title: A Bill Reserved for the Minority Leader to Introduce
plain_summary: This is a placeholder bill title that is being held for the Minority Leader of Congress to use when they are ready to introduce a bill. The Minority Leader is the head of whichever political party has fewer members in that chamber of Congress. No actual bill text or topic has been assigned to this slot yet.
key_provisions: No provisions available yet
Bill content has not been written or released
Title is being held as a reserved spot
who_it_affects: No one can be identified as affected yet because the bill has no content, topic, or provisions written into it.
status_plain_english: This bill is just a reserved placeholder and has no content, no votes, and no actions taken on it yet.

original_title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to provide certain line item veto authority to the President.
plain_title: Giving the President Power to Cut Specific Budget Items
plain_summary: This proposal would change the U.S. Constitution to let the President reject or reduce specific spending items in a budget bill, instead of having to accept or reject the entire bill. For example, if Congress passes a budget that includes money for a project the President thinks is wasteful, the President could cut just that part without vetoing everything else. Congress could still override the President's cut if two-thirds of both the House and Senate vote against it.
key_provisions: President can reduce specific spending items in budget bills
President must notify Congress within 10 days of making a cut
Two-thirds of both House and Senate can vote to restore the cut spending
who_it_affects: This would affect all Americans by changing how the government controls spending, giving the President more power to cut specific programs while reducing Congress's full control over the budget.
status_plain_english: This proposal was sent to the House Judiciary Committee to be reviewed, and has not been voted on yet.

original_title: To inform the Senate that a quorum of the House has assembled and of the election of the Speaker and the Clerk.
plain_title: House Notifies Senate of New Speaker and Clerk Election
plain_summary: This resolution is basically an official announcement from the House of Representatives to the Senate. It lets the Senate know that enough House members showed up to get to work, that Mike Johnson from Louisiana was chosen as the leader of the House (called the Speaker), and that Kevin McCumber was chosen to handle the House's official records and paperwork (called the Clerk). Think of it like the first day of school when a teacher takes attendance and announces who's in charge.
key_provisions: Confirms enough House members were present to officially start working
Announces Mike Johnson as the elected Speaker of the House
Announces Kevin McCumber as the elected Clerk of the House
who_it_affects: This procedural notice mainly affects members of Congress and government operations, as it officially kicks off the 119th Congress and establishes who is in charge of running the House of Representatives.
status_plain_english: This resolution was quickly approved without any debate or objection, meaning it is fully finalized.

original_title: Authorizing the Speaker to appoint a committee to notify the President of the assembly of the Congress.
plain_title: Telling the President That Congress Is Ready to Work
plain_summary: This resolution allows the Speaker of the House to form a small committee whose only job is to officially inform the President that enough members of Congress have shown up to start doing business. Think of it like taking attendance and then sending a note to the principal saying class is ready to begin. Both the House and Senate have to confirm they have enough members present before this message gets sent.
key_provisions: Speaker appoints a special committee to carry the message
Committee notifies the President that Congress has a quorum (enough members present)
Both the House and Senate participate in sending this notification
who_it_affects: This resolution mainly affects Congress members and the President, as it is a formal procedural step that signals the government is officially ready to conduct legislative business.
status_plain_english: This resolution was approved and the matter is officially closed — no further action will be taken on it.

original_title: Authorizing the Clerk to inform the President of the election of the Speaker and the Clerk.
plain_title: Officially Telling the President Who Won House Leadership Roles
plain_summary: When the House of Representatives elects new leaders, they are required to officially notify the President of the United States. This resolution formally lets the President know that Mike Johnson was chosen as Speaker of the House and Kevin McCumber was chosen as Clerk for the 119th Congress. Think of it like sending an official announcement letter to the President after an internal vote.
key_provisions: Notifying the President that Mike Johnson was elected Speaker of the House
Notifying the President that Kevin McCumber was elected Clerk of the House
Fulfilling the formal requirement to inform the executive branch of House leadership
who_it_affects: This mainly affects the formal workings of the U.S. government, ensuring the President is officially aware of who is leading the House of Representatives.
status_plain_english: This resolution was quickly approved by the House without any objections and is now complete.

original_title: Reaffirming that the United States is not a party to the Rome Statute and does not recognize the jurisdiction of the International Criminal Court.
plain_title: U.S. Says It Does Not Follow the International Criminal Court
plain_summary: This resolution is a formal statement by Congress saying the U.S. does not belong to or follow the rules of the International Criminal Court (ICC), which is a global court that can put world leaders on trial for serious crimes. It also criticizes the ICC for trying to arrest Israeli leaders, saying those arrest warrants are unfair. The resolution shows support for Israel's right to protect its leaders from international legal action.
key_provisions: Reaffirms the U.S. is not a member of the International Criminal Court
Condemns ICC arrest warrants issued against Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu and Defense Minister Gallant
Supports Israel's right to defend itself and its leaders from international legal challenges
who_it_affects: This resolution mainly affects U.S. foreign policy and Israeli government leaders, signaling that the U.S. will not cooperate with ICC actions targeting its allies like Israel.
status_plain_english: This resolution was sent to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs, where a small group of lawmakers will review it before deciding if it moves forward to a full vote.

original_title: Reaffirming the House of Representatives's commitment to ensuring secure elections throughout the United States by recognizing that the presentation of valid photograph identification is a fundamental component of secure elections.
plain_title: Congress Says Photo ID Should Be Required to Vote
plain_summary: This resolution is a formal statement from the House of Representatives saying they believe showing a photo ID when you vote is an important part of keeping elections safe and secure. It doesn't create a new law or change any rules — it's more like an official opinion statement. Think of it like a team putting out a press release saying they support something, without actually doing anything new about it yet.
key_provisions: Reaffirms House commitment to secure elections
States that photo ID is a key part of election security
Does not create any new laws or requirements on its own
who_it_affects: This resolution could affect all eligible voters, particularly those who may not have a government-issued photo ID, such as some elderly, low-income, or minority voters, if future laws are passed based on this position.
status_plain_english: This resolution was sent to a House committee for review and has not been voted on yet.

original_title: A concurrent resolution authorizing the use of the rotunda of the Capitol for the lying in state of the remains of the late James Earl Carter, Jr., 39th President of the United States.
plain_title: Allowing Jimmy Carter to Lie in State at the U.S. Capitol
plain_summary: This resolution gives permission for former President Jimmy Carter's body to be placed in the U.S. Capitol Rotunda so the public can pay their respects after his death. This tradition, called 'lying in state,' is a special honor reserved for important national figures like presidents. It was scheduled to happen from January 7 to January 9, 2025.
key_provisions: Permits use of the Capitol Rotunda for the ceremony
Sets the dates as January 7–9, 2025
Honors Jimmy Carter as the 39th President of the United States
who_it_affects: This affects the general public, who would have the opportunity to visit the Capitol and pay their respects to former President Jimmy Carter during the lying in state period.
status_plain_english: This resolution was fully passed and agreed to by Congress without any objections.

original_title: Providing the sense of the House of Representatives that the House should not adjourn until the annual appropriation bills within the jurisdiction of all the subcommittees of the Committee on Appropriations for the current fiscal year are enacted into law.
plain_title: House Should Stay in Session Until Budget Bills Are Passed
plain_summary: This resolution says that members of the House of Representatives should not take a break or go home until they have finished passing all of the government's yearly budget bills. Think of it like a rule that says you can't leave class until your homework is done. It's meant to pressure lawmakers to actually complete the budget on time instead of leaving it unfinished.
key_provisions: House members should not adjourn (take a break) until budget bills are passed
Applies to all annual appropriations bills for the current fiscal year
Expresses the official opinion of the House of Representatives
who_it_affects: This bill primarily affects members of Congress, pushing them to stay and do their work, which indirectly affects all Americans who rely on government-funded programs and services.
status_plain_english: This resolution was sent to the House Appropriations Committee for review and has not been voted on yet.

original_title: Recognizing the importance of access to comprehensive, high-quality, life-affirming medical care for women of all ages.
plain_title: Supporting Better Healthcare Access for Women Across America
plain_summary: This resolution is a formal statement from Congress saying that women in the U.S. should have access to good, caring, and complete healthcare. It does not create any new laws or programs on its own, but it puts Congress on record as supporting quality medical care for women. Think of it like a public declaration of values rather than a rule that must be followed.
key_provisions: Support for women having access to comprehensive healthcare
Emphasis on care being compassionate and high-quality
Focus on 'life-affirming' medical treatment for women of all ages
who_it_affects: This resolution affects women of all ages across the United States by expressing congressional support for their right to access quality and compassionate healthcare.
status_plain_english: This resolution was sent to a House committee for review and has not been voted on yet.

original_title: Disapproving of the rule submitted by the Department of Homeland Security relating to "Modernizing H-2 Program Requirements, Oversight, and Worker Protections".
plain_title: Congress Tries to Cancel New Protections for Temporary Foreign Workers
plain_summary: This bill would cancel a rule created by the Department of Homeland Security that was meant to give more rights and protections to temporary foreign workers who come to the U.S. on H-2A (farm work) and H-2B (non-farm work) visas. The canceled rule had given these workers things like protection if they reported unsafe conditions, equal treatment regardless of what country they came from, and 60 extra days to stay in the U.S. if they lost their job. By passing this bill, Congress would erase those new protections.
key_provisions: Cancel whistleblower protections for temporary foreign workers
Remove equal treatment rules based on workers' home countries
Eliminate the 60-day grace period after losing a job
who_it_affects: Temporary foreign workers on H-2A and H-2B visas — such as farm workers and seasonal workers — would lose recently granted protections and rights if this bill passes.
status_plain_english: This bill has been sent to the House Judiciary Committee, where it will be reviewed before any vote can happen.

original_title: Regarding consent to assemble outside the seat of government.
plain_title: Allowing Congress to Meet Outside of Washington, D.C.
plain_summary: This resolution gives Congress permission to hold its meetings somewhere other than Washington, D.C., if top leaders decide it is necessary. The Speaker of the House and the Senate Majority Leader can agree together to move Congress to a different location. They must first talk it over with the minority leaders from both parties before making that decision.
key_provisions: Congress can meet outside Washington, D.C. during 2025-2026
The House Speaker and Senate Majority Leader must agree it serves the public interest
Minority party leaders from both chambers must be consulted first
who_it_affects: This primarily affects members of Congress and their staff, since it changes where they are allowed to officially meet and conduct government business.
status_plain_english: This resolution has passed the House and been sent to the Senate, where it still needs to be voted on.

original_title: Electing officers of the House of Representatives.
plain_title: Choosing Key Staff Leaders for the U.S. House of Representatives
plain_summary: This resolution officially appoints three important staff members who run the behind-the-scenes operations of the U.S. House of Representatives. The Clerk keeps official records, the Sergeant-at-Arms handles security and enforces rules, and the Chief Administrative Officer manages the day-to-day business like payroll and technology. Think of it like a school electing its principal, head of security, and office manager all at once.
key_provisions: Kevin McCumber of Illinois appointed as House Clerk
William McFarland of Maryland appointed as Sergeant-at-Arms
Catherine Szpindor of Virginia appointed as Chief Administrative Officer
who_it_affects: This resolution affects the staff and operations of the U.S. House of Representatives, as these three officials oversee recordkeeping, security, and administrative functions that keep Congress running smoothly.
status_plain_english: This resolution has been fully approved and is considered final — no further action is needed.

original_title: A resolution informing the House of Representatives that a quorum of the Senate is assembled.
plain_title: Senate Tells the House It Has Enough Members Present
plain_summary: This resolution is basically a formal announcement from the Senate to the House of Representatives. It lets the House know that enough senators have shown up (a quorum) for the Senate to officially do its work. Think of it like taking attendance at school and announcing that class can begin.
key_provisions: Senate secretary sends official notice to the House
Confirms enough senators are present to conduct business
Signals the Senate is ready to start working on legislation
who_it_affects: This procedural action mainly affects members of Congress, as it formally allows the Senate and House to coordinate and begin conducting official legislative business together.
status_plain_english: The Senate passed this procedural notice and sent it over to the House of Representatives to officially inform them.

original_title: A resolution to elect Charles E. Grassley, a Senator from the State of Iowa, to be President pro tempore of the Senate of the United States.
plain_title: Choosing a Backup Leader for the U.S. Senate
plain_summary: This resolution officially selects Senator Charles Grassley of Iowa to serve as President pro tempore of the Senate. The President pro tempore is the Senate's official backup leader — they run Senate meetings when the Vice President (who normally leads the Senate) is absent. This is mostly a ceremonial role, but it also places the person third in line to become President of the United States.
key_provisions: Elects Charles Grassley as President pro tempore of the Senate
Gives Grassley authority to lead Senate sessions in the Vice President's absence
Places Grassley third in the line of presidential succession
who_it_affects: This resolution mainly affects the formal leadership structure of the U.S. Senate and has little direct impact on everyday Americans, though it does determine who is third in line to the presidency.
status_plain_english: This resolution was voted on and approved by every senator present on the same day it was introduced, with no changes made.

original_title: A resolution notifying the President of the United States of the election of a President pro tempore.
plain_title: Senate Officially Informs President Who Leads Senate Temporarily
plain_summary: When the Vice President (who normally leads the Senate) is unavailable, a Senator is chosen to fill that role temporarily — this person is called the President pro tempore. This resolution is simply the Senate's official way of letting the President of the United States know that Senator Charles Grassley from Iowa has been chosen for that job. Think of it like sending a formal notification letter to the boss letting them know who's in charge while the usual leader is away.
key_provisions: Officially announces Charles Grassley as President pro tempore of the Senate
Formally notifies the U.S. President of this leadership selection
Documents the Senate's choice for its temporary presiding officer
who_it_affects: This primarily affects the internal operations of the U.S. Senate and Senator Charles Grassley, who now holds a leadership role, but it has little direct impact on everyday Americans.
status_plain_english: The Senate unanimously agreed to this resolution right away, meaning every Senator present approved it with no debate or changes needed.

original_title: Fair Representation Amendment
plain_title: Limit Presidential Power and Restore Congress's Authority Over Laws
plain_summary: This resolution tells multiple House committees to look through all the laws they oversee and find places where the President and executive branch have too much freedom to decide how those laws are applied. The goal is to pull that power back to Congress, where elected representatives make the rules. All the suggestions from these committees would then be combined into one big bill called the 'Article One Restoration Act.'
key_provisions: 16 House committees must review laws in their areas
Committees identify where the executive branch has too much unchecked power
All recommendations get packaged into one new bill for Congress to vote on
who_it_affects: This bill would primarily affect how the President and federal agencies operate, potentially limiting their ability to make decisions independently and shifting more control back to elected members of Congress.
status_plain_english: This resolution was sent to the House Committee on Rules, which means it is in early stages and has not been debated or voted on by the full House yet.

original_title: HEALTH Act
plain_title: Creating a New House Committee Dedicated Solely to Health Issues
plain_summary: This bill would create a brand new official group in the House of Representatives called the Committee on Health, which would be specifically in charge of all health-related laws. Right now, health topics are split between two different committees, but this bill would move all of those responsibilities — like rules about the FDA, the CDC, and public health — into one focused committee. Think of it like reorganizing a school so that instead of two teachers each handling part of a subject, one dedicated teacher handles all of it.
key_provisions: Creates a new standing House Committee on Health
Gives the new committee authority over the FDA, CDC, and public health laws
Removes health-related duties from two existing committees
who_it_affects: This bill mainly affects how Congress operates internally, but could eventually impact everyday Americans by changing how health laws and regulations are developed and overseen.
status_plain_english: This bill has been sent to the House Committee on Rules, which means it is in very early stages and has not been voted on yet.

original_title: A concurrent resolution to provide for the counting on January 6, 2025, of the electoral votes for President and Vice President of the United States.
plain_title: Setting the Date to Officially Count Presidential Election Votes
plain_summary: After a presidential election, Congress must officially count and confirm the electoral votes to declare a winner. This resolution sets January 6, 2025 at 1:00 PM as the official date and time for Congress to do that counting. Think of it like scheduling the final, official announcement of who won the election.
key_provisions: Sets January 6, 2025 at 1:00 PM as the official vote-counting time
Results must be delivered to the President of the Senate
The final count must be recorded in the official journals of Congress
who_it_affects: This affects all Americans because it is part of the formal process that officially confirms who will become the next President and Vice President of the United States.
status_plain_english: This resolution was fully passed and agreed to by Congress without any objections.

original_title: A resolution electing Gary B. Myrick, of Virginia, as Secretary for the Minority of the Senate.
plain_title: Senate Picks Gary Myrick as Secretary for the Minority
plain_summary: This resolution officially appoints Gary B. Myrick from Virginia to a staff leadership role in the U.S. Senate. The Secretary for the Minority is a senior administrative official who helps the minority party (whichever party has fewer Senate seats) run its day-to-day operations. Think of it like electing a head office manager for one side of the Senate.
key_provisions: Officially elects Gary B. Myrick to the position
Fills the role of Secretary for the Minority of the Senate
Applies to the Senate's minority party operations
who_it_affects: This primarily affects Gary B. Myrick, who gets a new job, and the minority party senators who will rely on his administrative support.
status_plain_english: This resolution was passed immediately and unanimously by the Senate with no changes needed — all senators agreed to it on the spot.

original_title: A resolution notifying the House of Representatives of the election of a Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper of the Senate.
plain_title: Senate Officially Announces Its New Security Chief to the House
plain_summary: The Senate chose Jennifer A. Hemingway as its new Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper, which is basically the Senate's top security and operations officer. This resolution is just the Senate's formal way of letting the House of Representatives know about this appointment. Think of it like sending an official memo to a coworker in another department to keep them in the loop.
key_provisions: Officially names Jennifer A. Hemingway as Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper of the Senate
Requires the Senate to formally inform the House of Representatives about this election
Completes the official communication process between the two chambers of Congress
who_it_affects: This mainly affects Senate operations and staff, since the Sergeant at Arms oversees Senate security and day-to-day functions, but it has little to no direct impact on everyday Americans.
status_plain_english: The Senate passed this resolution and has officially sent notice to the House of Representatives, so it is essentially complete.

original_title: A resolution notifying the President of the United States of the election of a Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper of the Senate.
plain_title: Senate Officially Notifies President of New Sergeant at Arms
plain_summary: This resolution is the Senate officially telling the President that they have chosen Jennifer A. Hemingway as their new Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper. The Sergeant at Arms is basically the Senate's head of security and operations — they manage safety, maintain order during Senate sessions, and oversee many day-to-day functions of the Senate building. This is a formal notification, not a new law, just an official announcement.
key_provisions: Notifies the President that Jennifer A. Hemingway was elected to the role
Recognizes her official title as Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper of the Senate
Formally documents the Senate's internal leadership decision
who_it_affects: This primarily affects Senate operations and Jennifer A. Hemingway herself, who now officially holds one of the Senate's top administrative and security positions.
status_plain_english: The Senate unanimously agreed to this resolution right away, meaning it passed immediately without any changes or debate.

original_title: A resolution electing Jennifer A. Hemingway as Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper of the Senate.
plain_title: Senate Appoints Jennifer Hemingway as Its Head of Security
plain_summary: This resolution officially gives Jennifer A. Hemingway a top job in the U.S. Senate called Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper. This person is responsible for security, maintaining order in the Senate, and managing many of the Senate's day-to-day operations. Think of it like the Senate hiring a new head of security and building manager all in one.
key_provisions: Officially elects Jennifer A. Hemingway to the position
Assigns her the role of Sergeant at Arms and Doorkeeper of the Senate
Applies to Jennifer Hemingway from the state of Georgia
who_it_affects: This decision primarily affects Jennifer A. Hemingway, who gains a powerful leadership role, and indirectly affects Senate staff and visitors since she will oversee Senate security and operations.
status_plain_english: All senators agreed to this resolution without any debate or changes, so it passed immediately and is now finalized.

original_title: A resolution notifying the House of Representatives of the election of a Secretary of the Senate.
plain_title: Senate Notifies House About Their New Record-Keeper
plain_summary: The Senate elected a person named Jackie Barber to serve as Secretary of the Senate, which is basically the Senate's chief administrator and record-keeper. This resolution is simply the Senate's official way of letting the House of Representatives know about this appointment. Think of it like one office sending a memo to another office to say, 'Hey, we have a new person in charge of our paperwork.'
key_provisions: Jackie Barber was elected as Secretary of the Senate
The House of Representatives is officially notified of this election
A formal message is sent between the two chambers of Congress
who_it_affects: This mainly affects the internal operations of Congress, particularly staff and members who will now work with Jackie Barber as the new Secretary of the Senate.
status_plain_english: The Senate passed this resolution and sent an official notice to the House of Representatives informing them of the decision.

original_title: A resolution notifying the President of the United States of the election of a Secretary of the Senate.
plain_title: Senate Officially Notifies the President of Their New Secretary
plain_summary: The Senate elected a new person, Jackie Barber, to serve as the Secretary of the Senate. This resolution is basically an official announcement letting the President know about that election. The Secretary of the Senate is an important administrative official who manages records, paperwork, and other official duties for the Senate.
key_provisions: Officially notifies the President that Jackie Barber was elected
Confirms Jackie Barber as the new Secretary of the Senate
Formalizes the Senate's internal administrative appointment
who_it_affects: This primarily affects the workings of the U.S. Senate and its staff, as Jackie Barber will now oversee important administrative and record-keeping duties for the Senate.
status_plain_english: The Senate unanimously agreed to this resolution right away, so it has already been passed and sent to the President.

original_title: A resolution electing Jackie Barber as Secretary of the Senate.
plain_title: Senate Picks Jackie Barber as Their New Secretary
plain_summary: This resolution officially gives Jackie Barber the job of Secretary of the Senate. The Secretary of the Senate is an important administrative official who manages the Senate's records, finances, and day-to-day paperwork. Think of this person as the head office manager for the entire U.S. Senate.
key_provisions: Officially elects Jackie Barber to the role of Secretary of the Senate
Fills an important administrative leadership position in the Senate
Takes effect immediately upon agreement by all senators
who_it_affects: This primarily affects Senate staff and operations, as Jackie Barber will now oversee the Senate's administrative functions, records, and finances on behalf of all senators.
status_plain_english: Every senator agreed to this resolution without any debate or changes, so it passed immediately and Jackie Barber is now officially the Secretary of the Senate.

original_title: A resolution fixing the hour of daily meeting of the Senate.
plain_title: Senate Sets Daily Start Time to Noon
plain_summary: This resolution officially sets noon (12:00 PM) as the time the U.S. Senate starts its daily sessions. Think of it like a school deciding what time classes begin each day. It is a simple scheduling rule for how the Senate organizes its workday.
key_provisions: Senate daily meetings start at 12:00 PM noon
Applies to all regular daily Senate sessions
Agreed upon by all senators with no changes needed
who_it_affects: This primarily affects U.S. senators and their staff, since it determines what time they are expected to show up and begin official Senate business each day.
status_plain_english: This resolution was immediately agreed to by every senator without any debate or changes, so it is already in effect.

original_title: Small Business Prosperity Act of 2025
plain_title: A Bill to Help Small Businesses Grow and Succeed
plain_summary: This bill is called the Small Business Prosperity Act of 2025, but its full details have not been publicly summarized yet. Based on the title, it likely proposes new rules or tax benefits to help small businesses — like a local restaurant or family shop — make more money and stay open. Until more details are released, the exact changes it would make are unclear.
key_provisions: Likely includes tax relief or incentives for small businesses
May reduce financial or regulatory burdens on small business owners
Details of specific provisions are not yet publicly available
who_it_affects: This bill would most likely affect small business owners across the country, potentially making it easier or cheaper for them to run their businesses.
status_plain_english: This bill was recently introduced and has been sent to a House committee for review, meaning it has not been voted on yet.

original_title: Electing Members to certain standing committees of the House of Representatives.
plain_title: Choosing Which Representatives Serve on House Committees
plain_summary: This resolution officially assigns certain members of the House of Representatives to specific standing committees. Committees are small groups of lawmakers who focus on particular topics, like the budget or education, and review bills before the full House votes on them. Think of it like assigning students to specific class groups where they'll do detailed work before sharing results with the whole class.
key_provisions: Officially elects members to their assigned House committees
Fills open seats on standing committees
Formalizes committee membership through a House vote
who_it_affects: This primarily affects members of the House of Representatives, determining which lawmakers will serve on which committees and therefore which policy areas they will have direct influence over.
status_plain_english: This resolution has been fully passed and approved by the House, with no further action needed.

original_title: Rescinding the subpoenas issued by the January 6th Select Committee on September 23, 2021, October 6, 2021, and February 9, 2022, and withdrawing the recommendations finding Stephen K. Bannon, Mark Randall Meadows, Daniel Scavino, Jr., and Peter K. Navarro in contempt of Congress.
plain_title: Canceling Subpoenas and Contempt Charges Against Four Trump Allies
plain_summary: This bill would undo legal orders that forced four former Trump advisors — Steve Bannon, Mark Meadows, Peter Navarro, and Dan Scavino — to appear before the committee that investigated the January 6, 2021 Capitol attack. It would also erase the official findings that said these men broke the law by refusing to cooperate with Congress. Basically, it tries to wipe the slate clean for people who defied the investigation.
key_provisions: Cancel the subpoenas requiring four Trump allies to testify
Drop the contempt of Congress findings against all four men
Officially close out the contempt proceedings with no consequences
who_it_affects: Steve Bannon, Mark Meadows, Peter Navarro, and Dan Scavino would benefit directly, as their legal obligations and contempt charges from the January 6th investigation would be erased.
status_plain_english: This bill was sent to two House committees for review and has not been voted on yet.

original_title: Electing Members to certain standing committees of the House of Representatives.
plain_title: Choosing Leaders for House of Representatives Committees
plain_summary: This resolution officially selects the chairpersons who will lead 17 different committees in the House of Representatives. These committees are groups of lawmakers assigned to focus on specific topics, like agriculture, the military, or education. The chairs are the leaders who run the meetings and guide what each committee works on.
key_provisions: Electing chairs for 17 House committees
Assigning leadership across major policy areas like defense, budget, and judiciary
Formalizing committee structure for the current session of Congress
who_it_affects: This resolution affects members of Congress and their staff, and indirectly affects all Americans since these committee chairs will shape what laws get debated and passed.
status_plain_english: This resolution was fully approved by the House and is now considered final and complete.

original_title: Expressing the sense of the House of Representatives that the Parthenon Marbles should be returned to Greece.
plain_title: Should the UK Return Ancient Greek Sculptures to Greece?
plain_summary: This resolution expresses that the U.S. House of Representatives believes the United Kingdom should give back famous ancient sculptures called the Parthenon Marbles to Greece. These sculptures were taken from a famous Greek temple called the Parthenon in the 1800s and have been kept in the British Museum in London ever since. Greece has long argued the sculptures belong back in their home country.
key_provisions: Calls on the UK to negotiate with Greece about returning the sculptures
Acknowledges the sculptures were removed from Greece in the early 1800s
Supports the idea that the Parthenon Marbles belong back in Greece
who_it_affects: This resolution mainly affects the governments of the United Kingdom and Greece, as well as anyone who cares about cultural heritage and where historically significant artifacts belong.
status_plain_english: This resolution was just introduced and has been sent to the House Foreign Affairs Committee, where it will be reviewed before any further action is taken.

original_title: To repeal the National Voter Registration Act of 1993.
plain_title: Bill to End Easy Voter Registration Rules Across the U.S.
plain_summary: This bill would get rid of a 1993 law that made it easier for Americans to register to vote. That old law required states to let people register to vote at the same time they get or renew a driver's license — often called 'Motor Voter.' Without that law, states could make their own rules about how and when people can sign up to vote, which could make it harder for some people to register.
key_provisions: Eliminates the requirement to offer voter registration at DMV offices
Removes federal rules that standardized voter registration across states
Gives states full control over their own voter registration processes
who_it_affects: This bill would affect all eligible American voters, especially those who rely on simple registration options like signing up at the DMV, potentially making it harder for some people to get registered to vote.
status_plain_english: This bill was recently introduced and sent to a House committee for review, meaning it has not been voted on yet.

original_title: Improving Science in Chemical Assessments Act
plain_title: Making Chemical Safety Reviews More Organized and Efficient
plain_summary: This bill changes which offices inside the EPA are responsible for studying how dangerous certain chemicals are to human health. Right now, one specific EPA program handles these chemical safety studies, but this bill would shift that work to other EPA departments that specialize in those chemicals. It also creates a new committee to make sure different EPA offices aren't accidentally doing the same work twice.
key_provisions: Shifts chemical safety studies to specialized EPA program offices
Removes sole responsibility from the Integrated Risk Information System program
Creates a steering committee to prevent duplicate work across EPA offices
who_it_affects: This bill primarily affects EPA scientists and staff who conduct chemical safety reviews, and indirectly affects the general public who relies on those reviews to know which chemicals in their environment are dangerous.
status_plain_english: This bill was recently introduced in Congress and has been sent to two committees for review, but has not been voted on yet.

original_title: BRIDGE to Congress Resolution
plain_title: Letting Witnesses Testify Remotely in Congress Without Special Approval
plain_summary: Right now, if someone wants to testify at a congressional committee hearing from a remote location (like via video call), the committee chairperson has to give special permission first. This resolution would remove that requirement, making it easier for witnesses to participate in hearings without needing the chair's approval. For example, an expert living far from Washington D.C. could testify virtually without jumping through extra hoops.
key_provisions: Removes the requirement for committee chairs to approve remote testimony
Makes remote witness participation easier and more accessible
Changes existing House of Representatives rules on committee proceedings
who_it_affects: Witnesses such as experts, advocates, and ordinary citizens who want to testify before congressional committees would find it easier to participate remotely without needing special permission from the committee chair.
status_plain_english: This resolution was just introduced and sent to the House Committee on Rules, where it will be reviewed before any further action is taken.

original_title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States extending the right to vote to citizens sixteen years of age or older.
plain_title: Letting 16 and 17 Year Olds Vote in Elections
plain_summary: This proposal would change the U.S. Constitution to allow citizens who are 16 or 17 years old to vote in elections, including for president, Congress, and local offices. Right now, you have to be at least 18 to vote anywhere in the U.S. If this passed, millions of high schoolers could vote for the first time.
key_provisions: Lowers the minimum voting age from 18 to 16
Applies to all U.S. citizens who are at least 16 years old
Would require a change to the U.S. Constitution itself
who_it_affects: 16 and 17 year olds across the United States would gain the right to vote in elections for the first time, giving them a direct say in the laws and leaders that affect their lives.
status_plain_english: This bill was just sent to a House committee for review and has not been voted on yet.

original_title: Electing Members to certain standing committees of the House of Representatives.
plain_title: Choosing House Members to Serve on Four Key Committees
plain_summary: This resolution officially assigns certain members of the House of Representatives to four important committees. These committees handle big topics like government spending, energy, banking, and taxes. Think of it like assigning students to specific clubs or teams where they will focus their work.
key_provisions: Assigns members to the Appropriations Committee, which controls government spending
Assigns members to the Energy and Commerce Committee, which oversees energy and business rules
Assigns members to the Financial Services and Ways and Means Committees, which handle banking and taxes
who_it_affects: This primarily affects Members of Congress, determining which representatives will have decision-making power over major issues like the federal budget, energy policy, and taxes.
status_plain_english: This resolution has been fully passed and finalized, with no further action needed.

original_title: A resolution to constitute the majority party's membership on certain committees for the One Hundred Nineteenth Congress, or until their successors are chosen.
plain_title: Republicans Pick Their Senate Committee Members and Leaders
plain_summary: This resolution officially assigns Republican senators to their spots on 20 different Senate committees and names who will be in charge of each one. Senate committees are like specialized teams that study and work on specific topics — like farming, the military, or healthcare — before the full Senate votes on related bills. Think of it as setting the starting lineup for the Republican team in the Senate.
key_provisions: Assigns Republican members to 20 Senate committees
Names a chair (leader) for each committee
Sets up committee rosters for the 119th Congress
who_it_affects: This mainly affects Republican senators, who are now officially assigned to specific committees where they will review laws related to topics like healthcare, the military, farming, and more.
status_plain_english: This resolution was immediately approved by the full Senate without any debate or changes, meaning all senators agreed to it on the spot.

original_title: A resolution honoring the life and legacy of President Jimmy Carter and commending President Jimmy Carter for his life-long career of public service, humanitarian leadership, diplomacy, and courageous advocacy.
plain_title: Honoring Former President Jimmy Carter's Life and Legacy
plain_summary: This resolution is Congress's official way of paying tribute to former President Jimmy Carter after his death. It recognizes his decades of work both as president and as a private citizen doing things like building homes for the poor and promoting peace around the world. It also expresses condolences to his family.
key_provisions: Officially mourns the death of former President Jimmy Carter
Extends sympathy to the Carter family on behalf of the Senate
Honors his contributions to the United States through public service and humanitarian work
who_it_affects: This resolution primarily honors the memory of Jimmy Carter and offers comfort to his family, but it also serves as a formal recognition for all Americans of his contributions to the country.
status_plain_english: This resolution was voted on and approved by the entire Senate in one step, with every senator agreeing to pass it.

original_title: To terminate the designation of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan as a major non-NATO ally, and for other purposes.
plain_title: Removing Pakistan's Special Military Partnership Status with the U.S.
plain_summary: This bill would take away Pakistan's special status as a close U.S. military partner, which currently gives Pakistan access to extra American weapons and the ability to work on defense projects with the U.S. Pakistan could earn this status back, but only if it proves it has taken serious action against a dangerous terrorist group called the Haqqani Network, which is connected to the Taliban.
key_provisions: Remove Pakistan's major non-NATO ally designation immediately
Block the President from restoring Pakistan's status without proof of action against terrorists
Require Pakistan to arrest and prosecute Haqqani Network leaders to regain status
who_it_affects: This bill would primarily affect the Pakistani government and military, U.S. defense contractors who work with Pakistan, and indirectly, people in the region who are impacted by terrorism and U.S. foreign policy decisions.
status_plain_english: This bill was sent to a House committee for review and has not been voted on yet.

original_title: Stop Imposing Woke Ideology Abroad Act
plain_title: Bill to Cut Funding for U.S. Racial Equity Work Overseas
plain_summary: This bill would stop the U.S. government from spending money on a State Department official whose job is to fight racism and discrimination around the world. It would also block funding for the State Department's plan to address racial fairness in how it handles foreign affairs. Basically, it removes two programs focused on racial equity from the U.S. government's international work.
key_provisions: Ban funding for the Special Representative for Racial Equity and Justice
Ban funding for the State Department's Equity Action Plan
Applies to all federal dollars spent on these programs
who_it_affects: This bill would affect State Department employees and officials working on racial equity issues abroad, as well as communities around the world that benefit from U.S. efforts to combat systemic racism and discrimination internationally.
status_plain_english: This bill was introduced and sent to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs, meaning a small group of representatives will review it before the full House can vote on it.

original_title: Recognizing the 125th anniversary of organized Okinawan immigration to the United States.
plain_title: Honoring 125 Years of Okinawan Americans in the United States
plain_summary: This resolution officially recognizes and celebrates the 125th anniversary of people from Okinawa, Japan first immigrating to the United States. It honors the history and contributions that Okinawan Americans have made to this country over the past 125 years. It also encourages all Americans to recognize and celebrate this milestone.
key_provisions: Officially recognizes the 125th anniversary of Okinawan immigration to the U.S.
Honors the historic contributions of Okinawan Americans
Encourages Americans to celebrate this anniversary
who_it_affects: This resolution primarily honors Okinawan Americans and their communities by formally recognizing their history and contributions to the United States.
status_plain_english: This resolution was just introduced and has been sent to a House committee for review, where it will be examined before any further votes can take place.

original_title: Electing Members to certain standing committees of the House of Representatives.
plain_title: Choosing Which Representatives Serve on Key House Committees
plain_summary: This resolution officially assigns certain members of the House of Representatives to four important committees. These committees handle topics like the military, courts and laws, government oversight, and veterans' benefits. Think of it like assigning students to specific class groups where they'll focus on particular subjects.
key_provisions: Assigns members to the Armed Services Committee, which oversees the military
Assigns members to the Judiciary Committee, which deals with laws and courts
Assigns members to the Oversight, Veterans' Affairs Committees
who_it_affects: This mainly affects the members of Congress who are being assigned to these committees, as well as the Americans whose issues those committees handle, like veterans and military families.
status_plain_english: This resolution has been fully approved and finalized — no further action is needed.

original_title: Pregnant Women Health and Safety Act of 2025
plain_title: Rules for Doctors Who Perform Abortions and Abortion Clinics
plain_summary: This bill creates new rules that doctors and clinics must follow when performing abortions. Doctors would need to have a special agreement with a nearby hospital so their patients can be admitted there if something goes wrong, and they must tell patients which hospital to go to for follow-up care. Abortion clinics would also need to meet the same safety and building standards as outpatient surgery centers in order to receive any federal money.
key_provisions: Doctors must have hospital admitting privileges nearby
Doctors must inform patients where to get emergency follow-up care
Abortion clinics must meet federal outpatient surgery center standards to receive federal funding
who_it_affects: This bill would affect doctors who perform abortions, abortion clinics, and patients seeking abortions, by adding new legal requirements doctors must meet and new safety standards clinics must follow.
status_plain_english: This bill was recently introduced in Congress and has been sent to two committees to be reviewed, but has not been voted on yet.
